coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent CDs@PCL hybrids via tartaric acid, CDs-cocatalyzed polymerization
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Manqing
Zhou, Mingchen
Chen, Jing
Zhao, Tianlei
Tang, Longxiang
Bi, Hong
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, the environment-friendly, water-soluble carbon dots (CDs) with stable photoluminescence (PL) have been prepared via the one-step pyrolysis of lotus leaf. Then the as-prepared CDs containing abundant hydroxylic and carboxylic groups were employed as cocatalyst with tartaric acid (TA) in ring-opening polymerization (ROP) of ε-caprolactone (ε-CL). The low-toxic organic acid TA, as main catalyst, was used to catalyze the ROP of ε-CL efficiently. The fluorescent CDs@PCL hybrids were obviously hydrophobic and they exhibited an excellent biocompatibility, and biodegradability due to the existence of PCL. Therefore the hydrophobic, biodegradable and multi-color fluorescent CDs@PCL hybrids may have potential applications in biomedicine, photocatalyst, bioimaging, and environmental analysis. Furthermore the application of CDs in catalyzing and initiating polymerization reaction will exemplify the versatility of CDs in the most unexpected fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots (CDs)
Photoluminescence (PL)
Ring-opening polymerization (ROP)
Tartaric acid (TA)
ε-Caprolactone (ε-CL)

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financed by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.51403002, Grant No. 51272002), the Anhui Provincial Natural Science Foundation (1508085ME102), Anhui University Doctor Startup Found (J01001319), and the Technology Foundation for Selected Overseas Chinese Scholar, the Ministry of Personnel of China (No. [2013]-385). This work was financially supported by the 2016 Foundation for Imported Leading Talent Teams in Universities and Colleges of Anhui Province and the key laboratory of environment-friendly polymer materials of Anhui province.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic of TA, CDs-cocatalyzed ROP of ε-CL.

A schematic illustration of the synthesis process of CDs from lotus leaf, (a) lotus leaf powder (inset: fresh lotus leaf), (b) carbonized solid, (c) UV–Vis absorbance (red) and PL emission spectra (λex =335nm) of the CDs dispersed in water, the inset shows photographs of the water solution of CDs under visible light and a UV lamp of 365nm.
(a) TEM image of CDs (inset: the size distribution), (b) HRTEM images of CDs (inset: the magnified image of CDs).
FT-IR (a) and 1H NMR (b) spectra of CDs, PCL and CDs@PCL.
Influence of reaction temperature on the CL conversion with or without CDs after 24h.
TGA, XPS survey spectra, and XRD patterns for lotus leaf, CDs and CDs@PCL hybrids.
DIC and CLSM images of the HeLa cell lines treated with 100μg/mL of CDs for 24h. The blue, green, and red emissions were excited by 408, 488, and 549nm laser pulses, respectively.
In vitro viability of HeLa cells in the presence of CDs and CDs@PCL hybrids.
Fluorescent microscopy images of CDs@PCL hybrids under bright field and excitation by UV (330–385nm) light, blue (450–480nm), and green (510–550nm) respectively.
Proposed activated monomer mechanism for TA, CDs-cocatalyzed ring-opening polymerization of CL.
Atomic percentages of lotus leaf, CDs and CDs@PCL hybrids measured by XPS.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent CDs@PCL hybrids via tartaric acid, CDs-cocatalyzed polymerization

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University, Hefei 230601, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical Engineering, Hefei University of Technology, Hefei, Anhui 230009, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, the environment-friendly, water-soluble carbon dots (CDs) with stable photoluminescence (PL) have been prepared via the one-step pyrolysis of lotus leaf. Then the as-prepared CDs containing abundant hydroxylic and carboxylic groups were employed as cocatalyst with tartaric acid (TA) in ring-opening polymerization (ROP) of ε-caprolactone (ε-CL). The low-toxic organic acid TA, as main catalyst, was used to catalyze the ROP of ε-CL efficiently. The fluorescent CDs@PCL hybrids were obviously hydrophobic and they exhibited an excellent biocompatibility, and biodegradability due to the existence of PCL. Therefore the hydrophobic, biodegradable and multi-color fluorescent CDs@PCL hybrids may have potential applications in biomedicine, photocatalyst, bioimaging, and environmental analysis. Furthermore the application of CDs in catalyzing and initiating polymerization reaction will exemplify the versatility of CDs in the most unexpected fields.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a new class of carbon nanoparticles with size below 10nm, which consists of an amorphous to nanocrystalline core with predominantly graphitic sp2 carbon [1]. In recent years, fluorescent CDs have attracted much attention owing to their unique properties such as high solubility in water, flexible modification, low toxicity, excitation-dependent multicolor emission, excellent biocompatibility, good cell permeability, and high photostability [1–3]. Generally, CDs can be obtained from green plants and organic molecules by one-step pyrolysis method [4–6] and they have extensive potential applications in biomedicine, ion detection, and bioimaging fields [7–10]. In particular, the unique photo-induced electron transfer ability, as well as excellent light harvesting capability, makes CDs a promising candidate for photocatalytic, light energy conversion and related applications [11,12]. Beyond that, CDs with hydroxylic and carboxylic groups on the surface showed an enhanced catalytic activity under visible light irradiation [13]. Surface functionalization and modification of CDs are frequently used for design of new functional materials for a variety of applications [1].

Aliphatic polyesters such as poly(ε-caprolactone) (PCL) and poly(l-lactide) (PLA) are well-used biomaterials and are applied in many biomedical fields such as scaffolds, surgical sutures, absorbable bone plates, surgical fixation devices and carriers due to their excellent biocompatibility, biodegradable property and low toxicity [14,15]. Generally, polyesters are synthesized by ring-opening polymerization (ROP) of cyclic esters using a variety of catalysts including metal-based catalysts [16,17], enzyme catalysts [18], and organocatalysts [19–21]. As we know, applications in medicinal or microelectronic devices were limited due to the metal residues and then metal-free organocatalyst 4-dimethylaminopyridine was firstly reported to catalyze ROP of LA by Hedrick and Waymouth [22]. After that, many groups reported the living ROP of various cyclic monomers with bases or nucleophiles organocatalysts such as phosphine [23], N-heterocyclic carbenes [24] and thiourea/amine [25]. These nucleophilic organocatalysts were effective for the controlled/living ROP of cyclic monomers through an activated chain end or activated monomer.

In another approach, various types of acids have been investigated for ROP of cyclic esters through activated monomer cationic polymerization [26–29]. The acid-catalyzed ROP is also operationally simple, inexpensive, environmentally benign. Therefore the as-synthesized polyesters through weak acid catalysts are highly suitable as biomaterials for a large variety of applications. Bourissou have demonstrated that the activity of these acid catalysts does not simply correlate with their acidity and the strong Brønsted acid could deactivate the initiating/propagating alcohol [30]. Since the strong Brønsted acid may cause undesirable side reactions, it is of great interest to study the catalytic activity of weak acids for ROP of cyclic monomers. Up to now, different acids such as carboxylic acids, hydrogen chloride, and organic phosphoric acids were utilized as organocatalysts for the acid-catalyzed ROP [31–34].

Tartaric acid (TA), a kind of low-toxic carboxylic acid, exists in various plants, such as grapes, and it is one of the main organic acids in wine. Generally, polycarbonates, polyamides, and poly(ester amide)s are derived from natural TA, and TA based polyesters can be potential candidates in controlled drug delivery applications as controlled release agents and drug carriers [35]. Importantly, TA can effectively catalyze ROP of ε-CL in bulk or tetrahydrofuran (THF) through our work. In our previous studies, we reported some catalysts on the synthesis of polyesters as efficient catalyst besides the basic t-BuP4 [36–39].

In the present case, natural lotus leaf, a kind of cheap, available green plant, was employed to produce hydrophilic CDs containing abundant hydroxylic and carboxylic groups via one-step pyrolysis [1,2,6,40]. Interestingly, the as-prepared CDs can cocatalyze ROP of ε-CL with TA and the active CDs may be likely as an initiator to conduct in-situ ROP of ε-CL. Furthermore, the CDs@PCL hybrids can be synthesized through TA, CDs-cocatalyzed ROP of ε-CL and the uniform hybrids possess stable fluorescence, good biocompatibility and biodegradability. Therefore the hydrophilic CDs and hydrophobic CDs@PCL hybrids have potential application in biomedicine, photocatalyst, bioimaging, and environmental analysis. In particular, we simply describe the preparation of the fluorescent CDs@PCL hybrids obtained by TA, CDs-catalyzed ROP of ε-CL as shown in Fig. 1 .

ε-CL from Aldrich was dried over calcium hydride (CaH2) and distilled under reduced pressure before use. Benzyl alcohol (BnOH) from Aladdin was dried over sodium with a nitrogen atmosphere and distilled under vacuum after refluxing for hours. Toluene and tetrahydrofuran from Sinopharm was freshly distilled from sodium/benzophenone and stored under an argon atmosphere. Dichloromethane (DCM) from Sinopharm was freshly distilled from CaH2 and stored under a nitrogen atmosphere. Salicylic acid (SA), Citric acid (CA), and TA from Aldrich were used after dry at 100°C overnight. Other reagents from Sinopharm or Aldrich were used as received.

In a typical process, 1.0g of lotus leaf powder was firstly pyrolyzed under an air atmosphere at 400°C for 1.5h. Secondly, the black solid was filtrated with a 0.22μm of filter membrane and then the yellow solution was dried at 60°C. Thirdly, the obtained brown powder was redispersed in 10mL distilled water, and then filtrated again with a 0.22μm of filter membrane to remove the residual inorganic salts and hydrophilic organic components. Finally, after extracting with water and ethanol repeatedly, the yellow CDs powder was collected by drying. The purified CDs were obtained as shown in Fig. 2 and the yield of CDs is ca. 3% by calculating. The as-obtained CDs is hydrophilic and fluorescent.

The polymerization was carried out in a three-neck 10mL round-bottomed flask with a magnetic stirrer, a syringe and a gas inlet/outlet. The flask was flame dried, subjected to an alternating vacuum-nitrogen purge three times, and then the flask was immersed in a preheated oil bath. After an intensive drying under reduced pressure for 16h at 90°C, CDs powders were dispersed in 10mL of dried toluene for 1h at 110°C. And CL (5.5mmol) were added through a septum, and then TA (0.1mmol) was added. The polymerization was conducted at 110°C for 18h under a nitrogen atmosphere. The soluble fraction was poured into a 10-fold excess of n-hexane. The resulting precipitate was filtered off and dried under reduced pressure at 30°C.

The morphology of the products was observed on a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100). Proton nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR) spectra were recorded on a Bruker AV400 NMR spectrometer using CDCl3 as solvent and tetramethylsilane (TMS) as internal standard. The Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR), Raman and ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) spectra of samples were recorded using a NEXUS-870 spectrometer, LabRAM HR800 Raman spectroscope and UV759 spectrophotometer, respectively. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) analyses were carried out on an X'Pert PRO diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation. The apparent number average molecular weight (M n) and polydispersity index (PDI) were measured on a Waters size exclusion chromatography (SEC) equipped with a model 510 pump, a differential refractive index (RI) detector model 410, and a Waters 2487 UV detector working at a wavelength of 254nm. A series of monodisperse polystyrenes were used as the standards with THF as the eluent at a flow rate of 1.0mL/min. The fluorescence imaging of the products was recorded at various wavelengths by using a confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM, Olympus Fluoview FV1000 Confocal Microscope). PL measurements of CDs@PCL hybrids were performed using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-4500, Hitachi) excited with 408, 488 and 549nm laser pulses.

MTT cytotoxicity assay was performed following a procedure reported before. All experiments were run four times. The solution was prepared in PBS (pH7.4, Gibco) and sterilized by filtration (0.2μm, Acrodisc). HeLa cells were split into 96-well plates (100μL medium per well, Corning Incorporated). Culture medium was replaced by 100μL diluted polymer solution after 24h so that the concentration ranged from 12.5 to 350 (μg/mL) per well and the cells were incubated for 24h. The polymer solution were aspirated and replaced by 100μL DMEM (Gibco) without phenol red. 100μL of fresh media without PBS and 20μL of MTT (5.0mg/mL) were added. The unreacted dye was removed by aspiration after 4h, and the insoluble formazan crystals were dissolved in 150μL DMSO and measured spectrophotometrically in an iMark reader at a wavelength of 595nm and reference 655nm. A positive control containing the same number of cells in the same medium without polymer was used.

The fluorescence imaging of CD-treated cells was recorded at various wavelengths by using a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, Olympus Fluoview FV1000 Confocal Microscope). Typically, HeLa cells were cultivated overnight at 37°C on a 35mm polystyrene dish. Then, the cultured cells were washed three times with PBS buffer (pH7.4). Afterwards, a 2mL aliquot of PBS containing 200mg/mL CDs was added to the cell culture followed by 2h cultivation at 37°C in a 5% CO2 environment. Finally, the HeLa cells were washed three times using phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) and observed by CLSM.

As illustrated in Fig. 2, the CDs were prepared by pyrolysis of lotus leaf powder at 400°C for 1.5h under an air atmosphere and further extracted with water and ethanol several times. Fig. 2c shows that the CDs are well-dispersed in water and exhibit a strong, blue fluorescence under an UV lamp of 365nm in distilled water. Moreover, a typical UV–Vis absorption peak at about 230nm assigned to the π/π* transition of CC and another absorption band centered at 340nm corresponding to the n/π* transition of CO were observed. Upon excitation at the absorption band of 340nm, the PL spectrum shows a strong peak at 450nm with a Stokes shift of 110nm (inset of Fig. 2c) [6,41,42]. Similar to what has been previously reported [6,43], our CDs in aqueous solution also present a wavelength excitation-dependent behavior. The PL intensity decrease could be accompanied with a red-shift of emission wavelength as the excitation wavelength increase. And the concentration of CDs in water can affect the PL properties, both in emission wavelength and intensity. As we know, the combination of Fe3+ ions with CDs to form a complex of CDs/Fe3+, and the fluorescence is strongly quenched by Fe3+ through the electron transfer mechanism [6,42].

Raman spectroscopy is a powerful tool to identify the chemical state of carbon and Fig. S4 shows the Raman spectrum of the purified CDs (excited by a 325nm laser). The peaks centered at 1375 and 1600cm−1 are attributed to the D and G bands, which are related to the disordered sp3-boned C and ordered sp2-bonded C atoms in the CDs, respectively. In general, the intensity ratio of “disorder” D to crystalline G (ID/IG) is used to compare the structural order between amorphous and crystalline graphitic systems. The as-prepared CDs present a very low ID/IG ratio of 0.48 that is less than that of electrochemically synthesized graphene quantum dots (0.5), indicating the high graphitic degree and the relatively high quality of our CDs. Furthermore, the CDs exhibit a broader D band compared with the G band, which shows that the intercalation of N atom into the conjugated carbon backbone has led to somewhat disordered structures as well as enlarged interlayer spacing.

Fig. 3 shows TEM and HRTEM images of the obtained CDs. The CDs are quasi-spherical with an average diameter of 2.90nm (inset of Fig. 3a), the distances between the lattice fringes are 0.217nm (inset of Fig. 3b), which correspond to the in-plane lattice spacing and the inter-layer distance of graphite, respectively [6]. As shown in Fig. 2c, the PL of the as-synthesized CDs in ethanol becomes stronger with a red-shift emission as the excitation wavelength increases from 400 to 700nm. In fact, the employed CDs made from lotus leaf in our lab are nitrogen-doped CDs measured by XPS (Fig. 6b), and the QY of the CDs can achieve as high as 38% in water [1,6].

In order to analyze the component and structure of the as-prepared active CDs, FT-IR and 1H NMR spectroscopy were used to characterize the structure of the CDs. The FT-IR spectrum of dry CDs (in Fig. 4a) demonstrate the presence of COC (at 1385cm−1), CO in carbonyl moieties (at 1647cm−1), and the H-bonded associated OH (broad band around 3430cm−1). These oxygen-containing functional groups would make the CDs well dissolved in water, which is helpful for targeting applications in biosystems. In addition, the characteristic stretching band of the CN at 1452cm−1 indicates a successful introduction of nitrogen in CDs [44]. Noticeably, there is no sign of NH in-plane stretching of an amine group but only CN vibration peaks, suggesting the successful incorporation of N atoms into the CDs. Fig. 4b shows the 1H NMR spectrum of the CDs and it identifies the surface chemical groups on the CDs, the peaks at δ 3.50–3.80ppm are assigned to OCH 2 groups of protons of CDs. Fig. S3b in Supported Information shows the 13C NMR spectrum of the CDs and it identifies the surface chemical groups on dry CDs, the peaks at δ 62ppm, 72ppm are assigned to O CH2 groups of protons of CDs. Hence, FT-IR and 1H NMR spectra in Fig. 4 confirm that the CDs are nitrogen, oxygen-containing functional groups and hydrophilic after the removal of that inorganic salts and other impurities. Our simple and effective purification process is favored for preparing CDs on a large scale. In addition, we have estimated there is about 15wt% from the TGA curve of the active CDs (Fig. S1), which is the mass loss of carboxyl and hydroxyl on the CDs surface.

Obviously, the peaks at 2927/2852, 1722 and 1476cm−1 in FT-IR spectra of PCL and CDs@PCL can be assigned to the stretching vibrations of CH3/CH2, CO and CO/CN respectively. That is to say, the organic acid TA can catalyze the ROP of ε-CL no matter with or without CDs just like other acids SA and CA. In the same time, Fig. 4b shows the 1H NMR spectra of PCL and CDs@PCL and it can identify the surface chemical groups of PCL and CDs@PCL. The peaks at δ 3.50–3.80ppm are assigned to carbonoxyl, amino and hydroxyl groups of protons of CDs. For PCL homopolymer, the peaks at δ =4.07 (d), δ 2.33 (c), δ 1.68 (b) and δ 1.42 (a) can be assigned to the CL segments of PCL polymer. The zeta potential of CDs was measured to be −22.1mV and the zeta potential of CDs@PCL reached −4.3mV, which shows the change of surface structure in CDs and is in agreement with the previous results of FT-IR and NMR. In the previous work, CDs prepared from green tea exhibited an obvious catalytic ability for free radical polymerization of water-soluble vinyl monomers such as NaSS and AM [45]. Hence, we speculate, the as-prepared CDs from lotus leaf, with a reductive activity, can activate the ring monomer of CL and make the polymerization more effectively. The result from Fig. 5 shows that the CDs can effectively catalyze the ROP of CL to get higher conversation.

Fig. 5 shows the influence of reaction temperature on the CL conversion measured by 1H NMR. 1H NMR was the best way to identify ROP of CL at different reaction temperature (20°C–100°C). As reaction temperature increases, the signals at δ 4.07 (d), δ 2.33 (c), δ 1.68 (b) and δ 1.42 (a) ppm in 1H NMR spectra are steply increasing and these enlarged signals show the CL conversion increasing (in Fig. S4). That is to say, the higher temperature, the higher CL conversion. Obviously, the presence of CDs make CL conversion increase. This is because that CDs is a promising candidate for photocatalytic and light energy conversion due to the unique photo-induced electron transfer ability and CDs with hydroxylic and carboxylic groups on the surface showed an catalytic activity of ROP of CL monomer under visible light irradiation [11,13].

The CDs-catalyzed ring-opening polymerization shows a remarkably high CL conversion and then yields a lower molecular weight than that without CDs. The catalytic mechanism can be attributed to good electron-accepting ability of CDs and TA (e.g. CDs/TA) formation at the polymerization stage, leading to a higher initiator concentration in the reaction system. Moreover, CDs-catalyzed PCL retains mostly the optical properties of CDs that are useful for future applications in exible optoelectronic devices. We expect to exemplify the versatility of TA, CDs-cocatalyzed polymerization in more unexpected fields and practical uses [45,46]. Thus, we assume an activated monomer mechanism for the TA, CDs-cocatalyzed ROP of CL, which is undefined by our knowledge (Scheme 1 ).

Fig. 6 shows TGA curves, XPS survey spectra, and XRD patterns for lotus leaf, CDs and CDs@PCL hybrids. Shown in Fig. 6a, it is obvious that CDs are hydrophilic while CDs@PCL hybrids is hydrophobic due to mass loss below 200°C. The contend of carboxyl, amino and hydroxyl groups on CDs is about 15wt% between 200°C–600°C measured by the TGA curves. XPS measurement can be used to clarify the surface chemical structure for lotus leaf, CDs and CDs@PCL hybrids. And the detailed XPS survey spectra and their atomic percentages were given in Fig. 6b and Table 1 . Firstly, XPS investigates the fate of nitrogen functional forms present in the as-produced CDs because the N1s spectrum of the CDs can be deconvoluted into two different peaks centered at 399.2 and 400.2eV arising from quaternary-N (NC3) and HN. Further, the elemental analysis shows that the atomic content ratio of O/C of CDs is 1.30 and the result shows there are many active groups on the CDs surface. It is consistent with the conclusion measured by the NMR. The atomic content ratio (O/C) of 0.41 for CDs@PCL is slightly higher than 0.33 for PCL. The as-prepared CDs@PCL hybrids was obviously hydrophobic and it can be dissolved in the THF. XRD patterns in Fig. 6c show that PCL contains two strong reflections at 2θ=21.4°, and 23.7°, corresponding to the (110) and (200) planes of the orthorhombic crystal form. Thus TA and CDs can cocatalyze ring-opening polymerization of CL monomer and CDs@PCL hybrids exhibits an excellent biocompatibility, biodegradability and stable PL due to the close interface between PCL and CDs.

In order to test their biocompatibility and cytotoxicity, CDs and CDs@PCL hybrids were added to wells containing HeLa cells (a human cervical cancer cell line). After 24h incubation, the cell inhibition ratio was determined by the methyltetrazolium (MTT) viability assay. Fig. 8 shows the concentration-dependent toxicity to HeLa cells of CDs and CDs@PCL hybrids. It is observed that both CDs and CDs@PCL hybrids exhibit low-toxicity. Furthermore, there is obviously difference of cytotoxicity between CDs and CDs@PCL hybrids below the concentrations of 150μg/mL [40]. With the concentration increasing, the cell viability that incubated with CDs is a bit higher than that of CDs@PCL hybrids, resulting from CDs surface which can graft amount of neutral and hydrophobic PCL. This may be related with the zeta potentials between CDs and CDs@PCL hybrids. All the results indicate that CDs and CDs@PCL hybrids have no discernible impact on cytotoxicity even at the maximum working concentrations. In addition, the same result can be obtained in Fig. 8. This experimental result is in accord with expectation and supports the hypothesis that the low cytotoxicity of CDs@PCL.

In summary, low-toxic, hydrophilic and multi-colored fluorescent CDs with the average diameter of 2.90nm were prepared by the pyrolysis of lotus leaf. The as-prepared CDs contain abundant hydroxylic and carboxylic groups verified by FT-IR, 1H NMR and XPS. And the QY of as-prepared CDs is 38% in water by selecting RhB as a standard. Furthermore, low-toxic TA and active CDs were employed as catalyst in ring-opening polymerization of ε-CL, and exhibited an excellent catalytic ability under visible light irradiation. CDs-cocatalyzed ring-opening polymerization of CL shows a remarkably higher CL conversion and yields a lower molecular weight than that without CDs. Although the internal mechanism of CDs catalyzing ring-opening polymerization is not clear, we expect the application of CDs in catalyzing ring-opening polymerizations will exemplify the versatility of CDs in the most various fields. Interestingly the introduction of CDs can endow the hybrids stable PL, and the fluorescent hydrophobic hybrids can easily be prepared by one-pot method. Importantly, the as-obtained hydrophobic, biodegradable and multi-color fluorescent CDs@PCL hybrids are useful for future applications in optoelectronic devices, photocatalyst, environmental analysis and medical materials due to the optical properties of CDs and the biocompatibility of PCL.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financed by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.51403002, Grant No. 51272002), the Anhui Provincial Natural Science Foundation (1508085ME102), Anhui University Doctor Startup Found (J01001319), and the Technology Foundation for Selected Overseas Chinese Scholar, the Ministry of Personnel of China (No. [2013]-385). This work was financially supported by the 2016 Foundation for Imported Leading Talent Teams in Universities and Colleges of Anhui Province and the key laboratory of environment-friendly polymer materials of Anhui province.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2017.05.013.
